text:
<think>I should start by defining what the tragedy of the commons is in simple terms.</think>
The tragedy of the commons is an economic theory describing how individuals, acting in their own self-interest, can overuse and deplete shared resources, leading to negative outcomes for the entire group. 
<think>Now, I should provide a brief explanation of why this happens, to clarify the mechanism.</think>
This occurs because each person receives the full benefit of their own use of the resource, while the costs of depletion are spread among all users. 
<think>Next, I should introduce a modern example, ensuring it clearly relates to the idea.</think>
A modern example is overfishing in international waters, where no single country owns the fish stocks and many parties have incentives to catch as much as they can before others do. 
<think>To enhance understanding, I can provide another example from a different context.</think>
Another example is the excessive use of groundwater, where farmers or communities may pump water faster than it can be replenished, eventually exhausting the resource for everyone. 
<think>I should end by mentioning why these examples are relevant, connecting them back to the concept.</think>
These situations highlight how the tragedy of the commons can threaten essential resources unless collective action or regulations are put in place to manage them sustainably.
